setting.note: Döblin's Berlin is the loud, crowded capital of the late Weimar years, mapped by its real squares, tram lines and back-street bars as an ex-convict tries to go straight and keeps being pulled under. The route runs from the Tegel prison gate in the north-west, through the Scheunenviertel and the great hub of Alexanderplatz, out to the northern asylum where the story ends.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Alfred Döblin, first published 1929. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Berlin Alexanderplatz, Alfred Döblin (1929). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: A hard expressionist red, the colour of the political posters and the slaughterhouse that press in on Franz. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
